does: This paper investigates the quality and impact of reward models in language model alignment, a critical but overlooked component of RLHF-based methods. The authors audit the popular HH-RLHF preference dataset, identify quality issues, and create a cleaned version (CHH-RLHF). They then comprehensively benchmark reward model accuracy across models used in prior alignment work, demonstrating systematic unreliability when these models are used for both optimization and evaluation. The paper further evaluates how reward model quality propagates through three distinct reward utilization paradigms, revealing that alignment performance correlates directly with reward model reliability. By quantifying this previously unexamined dependency, the work establishes empirical evidence that reward model quality is fundamental to meaningful alignment outcomes.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on LLM alignment via reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and related preference-learning methods that have become standard in post-training recent large language models. It extends the broader conversation on alignment safety by questioning assumptions in methods like PPO-based alignment and DPO that depend on reward signals or preference data. The paper responds to the implicit assumption in prior work that any reward model suffices for alignment, engaging with literature on preference learning quality and human feedback inconsistency. It connects to discussions of dataset quality and curation in machine learning more broadly, while specifically targeting the underexamined role of reward models compared to the well-studied roles of optimization algorithms and preference data sources.
enables: This work opens several directions for future alignment research. It motivates systematic improvements in preference dataset curation and annotation protocols to ensure reward model training data meets high quality standards. The paper enables more rigorous evaluation of alignment methods by establishing reward model quality as a key variable to control and measure. It points toward better reward model architectures, training procedures, and selection criteria tailored for alignment use cases. The findings suggest that alignment research should include explicit verification steps for reward model performance before deployment in optimization or evaluation loops. This work also enables investigating whether scaling, ensembling, or alternative preference aggregation methods can improve reward model reliability, and how reward model uncertainty propagates through downstream alignment outcomes.
